items.q: What are James's four characteristics of mystical experience?
items.correct: Ineffability (beyond words), noetic quality (gives knowledge), transience (temporary), and passivity (feeling acted upon by something greater).
items.misconception: Religious experiences are just hallucinations caused by stress.
items.random_wrong: Religious experiences only happen during dreams.
items.why: James was a psychologist describing common features, not assessing truth. Students collapse the question of describing experience into the question of whether God caused it — distinct issues.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does karma mean in Hindu and Buddhist thought?
items.correct: The principle that actions have moral consequences shaping future experience or rebirth — not fate, but ethical cause and effect built into reality.
items.misconception: Karma is when bad things happen to bad people as punishment.
items.random_wrong: Karma is a god who decides what happens to you.
items.why: Students treat karma as cosmic justice meted out by a deity. In its philosophical origins it is impersonal moral causation — your own actions, not external judgement, shape your circumstances.

items.q: Are verification and falsification the same test of meaning?
items.correct: No — verification asks what observations would show a claim true; falsification (Flew, after Popper) asks what could count against it: a genuine assertion must rule something out.
items.misconception: Verification and falsification are identical principles proposed by the same philosopher.
items.random_wrong: Falsification says religious claims are true; verification says they are false.
items.why: Ayer's verificationism and Flew's falsification challenge are distinct tools from different debates (1936 vs 1950). Conflating them muddles who is arguing what in the University debate.

items.q: Was Hare's notion of 'bliks' an attack on religious belief?
items.correct: No — Hare offered bliks as a partial defence: religious beliefs are unfalsifiable framing convictions, yet still deeply meaningful because they shape how believers live.
items.misconception: Hare invented bliks to prove that religious people are insane like his lunatic example.
items.random_wrong: Hare argued that bliks are scientific hypotheses awaiting testing.
items.why: Hare conceded Flew's logical point but denied the conclusion: meaning does not require falsifiability. The lunatic example illustrates how bliks function, not a diagnosis of believers.
It's Language, Truth and Logic. “The Concept of Mind” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's John Wisdom. “Wittgenstein” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Flew's Theology and Falsification appear?
items.why: Exact answer: 1950. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
